summary: Jess Frazelle shares her college entrance essay to encourage others who may have been criticized for their unconventional writing style. She recounts how her satirical senior thesis about the moon landing being fake earned her first D, but she refused to compromise her authentic voice. The essay itself, 'Pharmy Tales,' is a collection of humorous vignettes from her high school job at a pharmacy, showcasing her natural storytelling ability and sardonic wit.
key_points: Received first D on AP English senior thesis for writing satirically about moon landing conspiracies
Her mom thought the unconventional pharmacy essay would prevent college acceptance; her dad loved it
Got accepted to NYU early admission despite (or because of) her authentic writing style
The pharmacy job provided endless material for storytelling
money_quotes: I like to think that I write how I speak. This was not always taken well, however. I tend to be a bit of a sarcastic troll.
Never be ashamed to be yourself and think differently.
I also value hearing all perspectives and then finding the truth somewhere in the middle.
Other than a large dose of feeling like shit (I am a perfectionist) I decided to not give any fucks.
Don't be fooled by the misconception that working in a pharmacy is dull. Everyday, I come home with a new story to tell.
key_insight: Authentic self-expression and unconventional thinking, even when criticized by authority figures, can be a strength rather than a liability.
